title: State of AI-assisted Software Development 2025
method: Global survey plus more than 100 hours of qualitative research
finding: 90% reported AI use at work; the main conclusion is that AI amplifies the strengths and weaknesses of the existing delivery system.
limitation: Cross-sectional and largely self-reported; it identifies associations and organizational patterns, not Wenova-sized causal effects.

title: Impact of Generative AI in Software Development
sample: Industry survey; exact analytic sample varies by model
finding: A 25% increase in AI adoption was associated with 1.5% lower delivery throughput and 7.2% lower delivery stability; DORA proposed larger batch size as a mechanism.
limitation: Observational association, not an experiment; the 2025 DORA report later described a changing relationship with throughput and instability.

title: Experienced open-source developer productivity RCT
method: Randomized controlled trial on real repository issues
sample: 16 experienced developers, 246 tasks, about 2 hours per task
finding: AI-allowed tasks took 19% longer; developers forecast a 24% speedup and still perceived a 20% speedup afterward.
limitation: Early-2025 tools, very experienced maintainers and mature open-source repositories; not an agent-ready multi-agent operating system.

title: Late-2025 developer productivity update
method: Follow-up randomized study with severe selection and time-measurement limitations
finding: Raw estimates suggested approximately 18% and 4% faster work for returning and new cohorts, but both confidence intervals crossed zero and METR judged the signal unreliable.
limitation: Non-participation, task selection and parallel-agent time accounting bias the estimate; use only as evidence that tool effects change quickly.

title: Three field experiments with software developers
method: Randomized field experiments at Microsoft, Accenture and a Fortune 100 company
finding: AI access increased completed tasks by 26.08% (standard error 10.3%); effects were larger for less experienced developers.
limitation: Coding-completion assistant treatment and enterprise task definitions differ from autonomous issue-to-evidence delivery.

title: GitHub Copilot task-completion experiment
finding: The Copilot group completed a JavaScript HTTP-server task 55% faster; completion was 78% versus 70%.
limitation: One bounded greenfield task with automated scoring; it does not measure production integration, review or long-lived repository work.

title: GitHub Copilot code-quality experiment
method: Randomized authoring task followed by blinded expert review
finding: Copilot access raised the likelihood of passing all tests by 53.2%; blinded ratings improved 2.47-4.16%, and approval likelihood rose 5%.
limitation: Vendor-run bounded Python exercise; percentage-likelihood and rating changes should not be read as production defect reductions.

title: Task-stratified AI-agent pull-request acceptance
method: Observational analysis of agent-authored GitHub pull requests
sample: 7,156 pull requests across five coding agents
finding: Task type explained more variation than typical agent differences: documentation PRs had 82.1% acceptance versus 66.1% for new features.
limitation: Public GitHub selection and acceptance are not equivalent to internal value, stability or cost; task mix is a major confounder.

title: Longitudinal study of AI coding-assistant work
finding: 84% perceived productivity improvement at both waves, while worsened developer experience in at least one dimension rose from 14% to 27%; work shifted from creation toward verification.
limitation: Self-reported perceptions and attrition; useful for supervisory-work hypotheses, not causal throughput estimates.
